model.purpose: project the top 100 players for the upcoming basketball season
model.newcomerBlend: 100% projection score (there should be somewhat of a learning/adjustment curve for freshmen)
notes: Unranked prospect with a credible high-major offer sheet
Walk-on or minimal Division I recruiting profile

players.projectionNotes: Glass is a young talent bet more than a proven high-major sure thing. Vanderbilt's own bio shows a massive freshman scoring profile at Washington State and strong recruiting pedigree. The SEC jump and limited Vanderbilt lineup evidence keep starter at Uncertain, but his development/upside score should be strong.

Strong role and starter signal. Indiana's roster confirms him, Indiana coverage highlights him as a major part of the roster rebuild, and SI projects him as the starting power forward. Burden stays below lead-creator territory because Indiana also has Markus Burton, Samet Yigitoglu, Jaeden Mustaf, and other pieces.

Condon is a high-confidence returning frontcourt star. Florida's roster context supports a major role, and his NBA/pro production sentiment is stronger than most returners. The score stays tethered to history but gives him a meaningful talent/role boost.

Lindsey was a true engine at UAlbany, leading America East in scoring while ranking near the top of the league in assists. Fordham's roster has returning frontcourt starters but less proven guard creation, so the role path is strong. I kept starter at Uncertain because I did not find a Fordham projected-lineup source or explicit signing article naming him a starter.

Meadow is a proven Mountain West starter and efficient shooter, but Oregon's roster is extremely crowded with transfers and high-end young talent. I see him as a useful rotation forward/shooter, but not a clear starter or major offensive burden player without lineup evidence.

Thompson scores as a premium freshman prospect with major NBA/upside signals. Ohio State's roster should give him enough early runway, but his role score is kept slightly below the freshman gold top tier until starting-lineup evidence is firmer.

Strong fit/role signal, but revised down from primary-star treatment. George Mason announced his return and explicitly framed him as someone who knows Tony Skinn's system. He was First Team All-SoCon after ranking second in that league in scoring, so the projection gives him a starter/major-guard opportunity but not a 90+ role without a lineup projection.

Barkley is a strong role/opportunity transfer because Grand Canyon added him after a productive ETSU season and has a clear path for him to be a featured forward. NBA/upside remains modest, so the BBPR is driven mostly by role and historical anchor.

McCoy's NBA/upside profile is high, and Michigan should have a real need for perimeter creation. His score sits just below the top freshman tier because the roster includes other major pieces and the starting role is more inferred than directly reported.

Elite shooter and obvious Baylor need-fit, but role should be capped as specialist/rotation until a projected lineup emerges. Baylor signed multiple transfers and has guard competition, so prior Liberty scoring does not automatically become a starting Big 12 role.

Westry is a premium role and talent projection. CBS projects him as Xavier's starting guard and says Xavier's success comes down to whether he can be the true catalyst. Xavier's own release says he led the American in assists and assist-to-turnover ratio and expects immediate impact. This is one of the few batch players who deserves a 90+ role score.

Good starter signal. CBS projects him as Stanford's starting guard, and Stanford's release says he can step in and help immediately after a massive Delaware role. The role is capped low-80s because Stanford also has a strong freshman/newcomer group and Bliss is moving up from CUSA/Delaware to the ACC.

Strong scorer and important early portal addition for a new NC State staff, but not an automatic starter because NC State added a deep transfer group with multiple guards. His Santa Clara production, First-Team All-WCC profile, and NBA-fit comments support meaningful role and upside.

Certa's late-season Notre Dame scoring spike is real, and Clemson specifically added him as an ACC-tested guard. The lower historical anchor keeps the BBPR modest, while the projection inputs reflect a plausible rotation scorer with upside but no confirmed starting role.

Bowser has direct projected-lineup evidence as Arkansas' starting center and an efficient, rim-running profile that fits with the Razorbacks' guard-heavy roster. The offensive burden is capped because Wilkinson, Richmond and high-end freshmen should drive more of the offense, but Bowser's size/finishing should earn a real starting or starter-adjacent role.

Smith brings real scoring credibility after winning NEC Player of the Year and averaging 20.7 points, but Rutgers has a fairly crowded group with returning guards Tariq Francis and Jamichael Davis plus frontcourt/wing additions Will Sydnor, Christian Gurdak and Lewis Duarte. I like him as a rotation scorer/top-six piece, but without a projected-lineup source he stays below likely-starter treatment.

Do not treat as normal transfer until eligibility is settled. He is reportedly returning to Florida but needs a fifth-year waiver. If eligible, his Kentucky production suggests a useful veteran rotation guard, but Florida's strong roster context and waiver uncertainty cap role/opportunity sharply.
Daubaris is more of an international development/rotation projection than an instant star. Cal's roster can create opportunity, but draft/upside signals are not as loud as the top internationals.

Fielder's profile is high-major friendly: 6-11, efficient inside, real shooting, All-Mountain West production. Alabama's own release praises his perimeter skill and immediate fit, and BamaHammer projected him into the starting lineup after his commitment. The cap comes from Alabama's crowded frontcourt with Brandon Garrison, Cole Cloer, Jamarion Davis-Fleming and other size, so he gets starter treatment but not primary-star opportunity.

Faulkner was a highly productive SoCon big with first-team and all-defense honors, so his historical anchor stays strong. The role is capped because Clemson added multiple transfers and has frontcourt competition, and Faulkner's non-shooting profile could make usage more matchup-dependent at the ACC level.

Strong scoring addition, but revised from likely-star to possible-starter/major-rotation. WVU added him after an All-Big East level scoring season at Butler, and WVU coverage describes him as a playmaker/scorer who could play guard or wing. The word 'could' matters: without a projected lineup, starter and opportunity change should be capped.

Major breakout freshman scorer with real upside, revised from projected starter to possible starter. TCU coverage says he could start at shooting guard next to Brock Harding. Give high talent for age/scoring profile, but cap role/opportunity until the projected lineup becomes firmer.

This looks more like a returning-star profile than a normal transfer. Cal Poly's roster confirms him back, and 2025-26 data shows he led the Big West in scoring while starting 30 games. Role and burden are elite; opportunity change is only moderate because he is staying in the same team context rather than gaining a new role.

Estrella has a strong historical anchor and a clean Michigan frontcourt fit, but starter status is kept Uncertain because direct lineup evidence is limited. His role can still be high because Michigan needs size and he has high-major experience.

Holcombe has direct starter evidence from Utah On SI and was UVU's top two-way player. Utah needed a roster rebuild and his size/defense/playmaking fit a major role. The score is high, but not 90+, because shooting limitations and Big 12 translation create some cap.

High-floor two-way guard with strong production and coach endorsement, revised lower on starter/opportunity because USC added multiple transfers and the batch did not find a projected lineup. Strong historical rating still supports a high projected BBPR even with moderated role inputs.

Crowe is a five-star/elite-scoring freshman with a clear immediate role at Missouri. The role is high, but not 100, because freshman guard efficiency and Missouri's veteran context create some translation risk.

Richardson is a useful high-major freshman bet, but Alabama's roster is crowded with older transfers and frontcourt/wing competition. His development score helps, while role/opportunity are capped until he has clearer rotation reporting.

Wilkinson gets one of the stronger scores here because there is direct projected-lineup evidence from SI and Arkansas is bringing in an SEC-proven scorer who led Georgia at 17.4 ppg. I stop short of 90+ because Arkansas has elite freshman talent and multiple athletic guards/wings who can claim touches, but he should be a high-minute, high-usage piece.

Burks started every game for an NCAA Tournament UCF team and gives Oklahoma State size, shooting and versatility. Oklahoma State also added a large portal class and several freshmen, so I like him as a starting-caliber or top-six forward but not enough to call him a definite starter without lineup evidence.

This is the clearest usage winner in the batch. Wichita State specifically targeted Frison to organize the offense, school coverage describes him and Jahari Long as key pieces in remaking the offense, and local reporting says he is poised to run the show. He is still not an NBA-upside play, but his role/opportunity should be very high.

Halaifonua has intriguing talent/upside as a former top recruit and efficient 7-footer. Oklahoma State's own release emphasizes his size, skill and feel. The role is capped because Oklahoma State has many new frontcourt pieces and Burks/Grbovic/Allmond create competition.

Neely looks like a very real opportunity play. Colorado lost its top three players and the Denver Gazette described Neely as the Buffs' only real portal splash, while Tad Boyle specifically highlighted rebounding as a major need. His NBA score stays modest, but role and opportunity are high because the roster needs exactly what he does.

Holmes is a strong Big East opportunity play. DePaul signed him as an experienced all-conference guard after a 19.5 ppg sophomore season, and the Blue Demons' roster still needs proven scoring. I stopped at Uncertain because DePaul added many guards, but role/offensive burden should be meaningful.

Taylor is one of the better talent-upside bets in this batch. Florida State describes him as a high-upside creator, he was a four-star portal player, and he was invited to the Jayson Tatum Elite Camp. FSU has other wings and transfers, so starter remains Uncertain, but he should be a major piece.

Dawes has strong starter evidence: Kansas' own bio calls him an immediate-impact veteran, Bill Self praised his versatility, and SI projected him into likely starting combinations. His opportunity is capped because Kansas has Tyran Stokes, Taylen Kinney, Leroy Blyden Jr. and other scoring/creation pieces, so his role is more versatile starter than primary option.

Kennedy has one of the best opportunity profiles in this batch. Wake Forest is in a near-total rebuild, and recent ACC preview coverage names him as a key player. He was both MAAC Player and Rookie of the Year as a freshman, so the development/upside path is real. I left starter as Uncertain only because no projected lineup was found.

Mincy projects as a useful veteran guard/creator for South Carolina. The role score is strong because of roster need and prior production, but limited NBA/upside keeps the total score from climbing too far.

D'Augustino is a clear MAC role/opportunity case despite a low historical anchor. Ohio's announcement frames him as a homecoming scorer, and he led IU Indy in scoring/minutes with efficient splits. The starter call is an inference from production and level fit rather than a published lineup.

High-upside young guard with strong Kansas coach endorsement. Bill Self called him one of the best guard prospects in the portal and said he can be lead/scoring guard, but Kansas also has major incoming backcourt talent. Treat as high-minute possible starter, not automatic lead guard.

Diop is a high-upside Gonzaga frontcourt projection. Gonzaga signed him after a strong freshman season at Arizona State, On3 rates him extremely highly, and current Gonzaga coverage treats him as a key frontcourt piece next to Braden Huff. His NBA/upside score is the highest in the batch; offensive burden is capped because Huff is still the frontcourt headliner.

High-level shooter/scorer stepping up from the Patriot League, revised lower because Maryland guard depth lowers certainty on starter status and offensive burden. Strong shooting can earn minutes, but not enough sourced lineup evidence for a 75 role.

Nwoko has direct starter evidence from the CBS Big East projected lineup and a clean role fit as Xavier's physical center. He started 29 games at LSU and Pitino expects immediate impact. Opportunity is high, but offensive burden stays below Westry/Milicevic levels because Xavier has multiple creators and frontcourt scorers.

Ruzic has a stronger immediate-role signal than most internationals because he brings professional experience and LSU has a clear need for frontcourt talent. Talent is very strong, but not quite elite one-and-done tier.

Muurinen is a major international/upside prospect, but Arkansas has one of the most crowded high-end rosters in the country. Talent is high; role is capped until preseason lineup evidence separates him from Arkansas' other forwards.

Strong starter/lead-guard signal. CBS projects him in Boston College's starting lineup, BC coverage frames him as a main offensive focal point, and Boston College underwent major roster turnover under a new coach. The high-major jump keeps him below true 90+ star treatment, but opportunity is legitimately strong.

Krivas projects as Arizona's veteran frontcourt anchor if healthy. His historical rating is already strong, and Arizona's roster gives him a starter path, but offensive burden is capped because Arizona has multiple talented perimeter and freshman options.

Allenspach is a productive, efficient frontcourt connector, but Cincinnati's roster has multiple bigs and forward options including Tyler Tejada, Jayden Hastings and Deshaun Vaden. I see a real rotation path and possible starter path, but not enough evidence for a high opportunity jump.

Treat as a likely high-minute guard, but not an automatic starter until SMU lineup context is confirmed. SMU lists him on the 2026-27 roster after a Tulane season where he started every game, led the team across scoring/rebounds/assists/steals, and played 36.1 minutes. The ACC/high-major jump and lack of direct projected-lineup evidence cap starter/role/opportunity.

Beasley has a strong path to major minutes because Washington specifically targeted his speed, paint pressure and scoring. The Huskies' own announcement says he was one of their top point guards on the board and that they needed his scoring in the Big Ten. I kept starter at Uncertain because I did not find a projected lineup, but the role/opportunity profile is clearly above ordinary rotation level.

Elite shooting production at Buffalo gives Sabol a clear specialist path, but Providence's roster has multiple high-usage or higher-upside perimeter pieces, including Dink Pate, Malik Mack, Gavin Hightower, Miles Byrd and Ryan Mela. RotoWire explicitly framed him as a sneaky shooter off the bench, so starter/role/opportunity are capped despite the strong shooting history.

Orme brings valuable shooting/forward production to Nebraska, but the Big Ten jump and Nebraska's returning pieces cap the projection. He is a likely rotation or starter-adjacent forward, not a clear primary option.

Demary is a strong returning-star projection. UConn added him as a centerpiece guard, and roster context points to a high-minute, high-usage role. The BBPR stays close to his historical anchor because he already owns a strong profile, but the role and offensive burden should be top-tier.

Strong experience and production, but starter status should stay uncertain because USF added a full 15-man roster with multiple guards and four-star talent. Wilson's 98 starts and 17.0/4.6 profile support major minutes, but the team-context cap keeps role under 80 until a projected lineup confirms him.

Top-end researched projection, but revised down from 90+ role because Illinois has other important pieces and the exact lineup hierarchy still needs confirmation. ESPN called him a top-10 transfer, and Illinois describes him as one of the best offensive players in the portal. He helps replace Illinois' starting backcourt after a Final Four season, with size, shooting, and playmaking upside.

This is a mid-major role-up case. Staveskie has multiple seasons of high-usage guard production, returns to Ohio, and Akron's transfer tracker shows him as a major incoming guard. The historical BBPR stays modest, but role/opportunity should be high in the MAC.

Dowd is a winning, productive transfer with rebounding and multi-position defense that Auburn clearly values. Auburn's frontcourt is packed with Owen Freeman, Bukky Oboye, Adam Olsen and others, so the opportunity is capped despite the double-double track record.

Strong Weber State production and usage, but the sheet has no upcoming team. A July 2026 Gonzaga roster-needs article lists him as an available option, not as a committed player. Do not score final opportunity until destination is known.

Riley is a veteran offensive creator joining a Cincinnati roster that was reset under Jerrod Calhoun. His Tulsa season was productive, but Cincinnati has Myles Colvin, Elijah Perryman, Grant Darbyshire and other guards, and the efficiency concerns noted by local coverage make me stop short of a huge score.

Clear elite-shooter addition, revised slightly lower because Tennessee added a large transfer group and his likely usage is more specialist/spacing than primary creator. Give solid minutes, uncertain starter status, lower burden, and low NBA/upside scores despite high college value.

Tanner gets an elite returning-player score because the historical anchor is already exceptional and the role path at Vanderbilt is enormous. The projection is slightly below his historical rating, not because of a role concern, but because the formula is conservative after an extreme historical anchor.

Versatile, productive forward stepping up to Cincinnati. His Towson production is strong and Cincinnati's release praises positional versatility, but no projected-lineup source was found and Cincinnati's 2025-26 roster context includes multiple frontcourt/wing pieces. Cap as strong rotation/possible starter.

Weeks is a proven scorer moving into a Memphis roster that needed transfer help after a down season. His Southern Miss usage was huge and Memphis coverage frames him as a major addition. Starter is still Uncertain because the Tigers have a large transfer class and I did not find a reliable projected lineup.

McClendon is a veteran guard joining a Pepperdine rebuild under Griff Aldrich. The upside is role opportunity at a lower level after high-major/mid-major stops, but his injury history keeps confidence and starter certainty lower.

Strongest role/talent signal in this batch, but trimmed slightly to account for other projected Kentucky stars. Kentucky signed him as a major portal addition at point guard after he started 29 games at Washington. Current coverage describes him settling in as Kentucky's starting point guard, so role, opportunity, and burden remain high.
